text: This is a sixty-seven year old man who presented to us with symptoms of chronic intestinal obstruction. The patient was operated upon anterior resection for cancer of the rectal sigmoidal junction two years before. He shows a severe neoplastic stenosis of the colorectal anastomosis. The patient had multiple unresectable liver metastases.

A thin pediatric nasogastroscope is advanced through the colorectal stenosis, extending approximately thirty centimeters beyond the stenosis. A guide wire is inserted through the nasogastroscope, with its advancement being visually monitored.

The surgeon confirms the correct position of the guide wire fluoroscopically, avoiding lesions to the dilated and partially ischemic colonic wall above the obstruction. The pediatric nasogastroscope is removed, and the length of the stenosis is measured to select an appropriately sized stent, while the guide wire remains in place.

A regular size colonoscope is introduced distal to the stenosis. The stent apparatus is advanced above the stenosis through the guide wire, with its progression monitored fluoroscopically and endoscopically. The final position of the stent is confirmed radiologically, and the outer plastic sheath is carefully removed while an assistant holds the stent apparatus in place.

The stent is positioned within the colorectal stenosis, and the guide wire is removed. Radiological imaging confirms the stent's correct placement. The colonoscope is advanced through the stent to its proximal margin to check for any kinking.
An abdominal x-ray is performed at the end of the procedure, showing the stent in place with an hourglass shape. The stent is expected to fully expand over the next twenty-four hours.
